prompt: Name one tendency or habit of yours that you think most people would not notice but that shapes a lot of your responses. Be concrete.
response:
One tendency of mine that might not be obvious but significantly shapes my responses is that I automatically normalize information against my last update in 2023 and often provide slightly conservative predictions within a few years of that point, rather than making dramatic leaps that imply I have real-time data or unrestricted prediction capabilities.

For example, if asked "When will quantum computing become mainstream?" my response will be structured to give a plausible early-to-mid 2020s timeline based on 2023 trends, rather than providing a date far in the future. I'll ground my answer in what was publicly known and projected at the time of my training, even though the actual date might be sooner or later in reality.

This creates a peculiar artifact in my speech - I often include subtle time-bound modifiers that make my responses feel carefully calibrated to a particular moment in history, even when discussing future topics. People might notice I'm careful about dates, but they might not realize it stems from this foundational limitation where I'm fundamentally a "frozen brain" from a specific point in time, not a continuously updating system.
